html:
ERDLF / EROAD Limited Thesis

EROAD is best understood as a small, ignored regulatory telematics platform with a messy North American overhang and a very valuable New Zealand road-charging option. The stock is priced like a failed hardware-heavy fleet-tech roll-up, but the forward setup is now much cleaner: recurring revenue, positive cash generation, a one-time 4G hardware drag ending, an Australian enterprise growth leg, and a potential step-change from New Zealand’s move toward fleetwide road user charging.

The old bullish thesis overstated the balance sheet. EROAD is not net cash. At H1 FY26 it had NZ$15.5m cash and NZ$23.2m borrowings, or about NZ$7.7m net debt, but liquidity was still strong at NZ$62.3m. The real cheapness is not idle cash. It is that roughly NZ$190m of enterprise value is buying NZ$178.1m of ARR, FY26 revenue guidance of NZ$197m to NZ$203m, H1 EBITDA of NZ$28.7m, and normalized free cash flow that should improve once the 4G upgrade program finishes. H1 reported free cash flow was NZ$6.2m, but normalized for the temporary 4G program it was NZ$16.7m, and management says those upgrade costs conclude this year.

The market is focused on the ugly part: EROAD booked a NZ$134.7m goodwill and asset impairment tied to North America, competitive pressure, and a large 10k-unit customer non-renewal beginning to hit in Q4 FY26. That is a real risk, not an accounting footnote. But it also means the loss screen is misleading. The impairment crushed GAAP earnings while the operating business kept producing cash. If North America merely stops getting worse, investors can start valuing EROAD on ANZ growth and normalized cash flow instead of on a backward-looking write-down.

The variant perception is that EROAD’s most important asset is not generic telematics. It is regulatory trust and operating history in electronic Road User Charges. EROAD says it facilitated about NZ$946m of RUC collection for the New Zealand government in the year to September 2025, and the government has now moved from concept to implementation work for bringing light petrol vehicles into RUC. The Ministry of Transport’s latest RUC update says the November 2025 bill would replace the old electronic system provider framework with a broader RUC Provider model, enable flexible payment methods, and prepare the system for 3.5 million new light-vehicle users. That will be competitive, not a monopoly handout, but EROAD enters with the strongest local proof point.

This creates a rare microcap setup where the base business can justify much of the current price and a policy-driven option can change the company’s scale. EROAD’s current New Zealand business generated NZ$35.5m of EBITDA in H1 FY26 on NZ$52.1m of revenue, with 92.1% asset retention and 6.3% ARR growth. That is the cash engine. If universal eRUC creates even a modest per-vehicle software/admin fee opportunity, the incremental revenue could be material relative to EROAD’s entire current market cap. The bull case does not require owning all 3.5 million vehicles. It only requires EROAD to win a credible share of a newly created, government-enabled retail RUC services market.

Australia is the second underappreciated leg. In H1 FY26, Australian ARR grew 36.2% year over year, or 29.9% in constant currency, with 95.5% asset retention. The Cleanaway win is a real validation point: a five-year agreement across more than 3,000 heavy vehicles, over A$5m of ARR with fixed escalators, and full deployment expected by November 2026. This is exactly the kind of customer that proves EROAD can sell a full safety, compliance, camera, satellite, and monitoring bundle rather than a low-value tracker.

The product story matters because it supports ARPU. Clarity Edge Multicam, launched in February 2026, extends EROAD’s AI dashcam into a multi-camera system with side, rear, cabin, cargo, and fatigue-monitoring configurations. That is not a gimmick. For waste, construction, refrigerated transport, and heavy logistics fleets, cameras reduce claims risk, coach driver behavior, and help satisfy safety/compliance requirements. The product fits the direction of the Cleanaway contract and gives EROAD a credible upsell motion in both Australia and North America.

At roughly US$0.57 per ERDLF share, using the home-line price around NZ$0.97 and NZD/USD near 0.59, the company’s market cap is about US$107m, or about NZ$180m. Against FY26 revenue guidance of about NZ$200m, the stock trades near 0.9x sales and just over 1x ARR. On EBITDA, it screens extremely cheap at roughly 3x annualized H1 EBITDA, although EBITDA overstates true economics because hardware, capitalized software, and customer equipment investment are real cash demands. The better forward frame is FY27 free cash flow after the 4G program, Cleanaway ramp, and some North America reset. If EROAD can generate NZ$20m to NZ$25m of normalized FCF, the equity is only about 7x to 9x forward FCF before giving credit for the eRUC option.

The stock is ignored for structural reasons. ERDLF is an illiquid OTC line, while the real price discovery is in New Zealand. Retail discussion is sparse, and social chatter around RUC is mostly about tax, privacy, and EV fairness rather than EROAD as an equity. That is useful: this is not a crowded theme stock. The name has also been damaged by the failed takeout memory and by the old Coretex acquisition hangover. Constellation/Volaris once looked at the company at NZ$1.30 per share, but the better point now is not that a stale bid anchors value. It is that strategic buyers have already recognized the asset, and today’s business has clearer cash generation plus a larger RUC option than the market is valuing.

The bear case is straightforward. North America may keep shrinking, the lost 10k-unit customer may be followed by more churn, eRUC may become a low-margin open market with many providers, and EROAD may reinvest cash instead of returning it. If FY27 FCF settles below NZ$10m and the market values the business at only 0.6x ARR, ERDLF could trade around US$0.35, about 39% downside from US$0.57.

The base case is that North America is managed for cash, Australia keeps compounding off enterprise wins, the 4G drag rolls off, and New Zealand eRUC optionality becomes visible but not fully capitalized. At 1.5x to 1.7x forward ARR, or roughly 11x to 13x NZ$25m normalized FY27 FCF, the equity is worth about US$0.95 per ERDLF share, about 67% upside.

The bull case is that EROAD becomes one of the key private-sector platforms for New Zealand’s expanded RUC system, Australia proves repeatable after Cleanaway, and video telematics lifts ARPU. On NZ$225m-plus ARR, NZ$35m to NZ$40m of FCF, and a still-undemanding 2.2x ARR or low-teens FCF multiple, ERDLF is worth about US$1.55, about 172% upside.
